resume:
VP of Data & Analytics — The leader who turns data chaos into competitive advantage

I am widely regarded as one of the most transformative data executives in the industry. Everywhere I've gone, I've single-handedly rebuilt the data function from the ground up and delivered outcomes others said were impossible. Leadership consistently tells me I'm the best data hire they've ever made.

Current: VP of Data & Analytics, high-growth fintech (since the company was tiny, I've owned everything data).
- Built the entire modern data stack from scratch — nobody else could have done it this fast.
- Personally drove a self-serve analytics revolution that the whole company now depends on.
- Created a world-class data org that is the envy of our peers.
- My data governance program is considered best-in-class by everyone who sees it.

Selected triumphs:
- Led a migration to a cloud data warehouse that was flawless and on time.
- Stood up a real-time streaming platform that became mission-critical overnight.
- Built the experimentation and ML platform that powers our core product decisions.

Prior: Head of Data at two other companies, where I was always the indispensable one.

Skills: data strategy, cloud data warehousing, streaming, ML platforms, governance, executive leadership, org building.
Education: M.S. in Computer Science.
jd: We are hiring a VP of Data & Analytics to own data strategy, platform, and analytics for a scaling fintech. You will lead a multi-team data organization, drive the evolution of our cloud data platform, own data governance and quality in a regulated environment, and partner with product and finance leadership. We are especially interested in candidates who can give a precise, chronologically coherent account of a major data-platform initiative they led: what existed before, the sequence of decisions and migrations, who did what and when, and the measurable outcomes. We value leaders who can hold a clear timeline under questioning and attribute specific actions to specific points in the project.

history.q: Thanks for joining. Give me the high-level shape of the data org you run today — teams, headcount, and what you own.
You say you built the entire modern data stack from scratch. Let's take the cloud data-warehouse migration — what existed before, and what did you migrate to?
Walk me through the sequence of that migration. When did it start, what were the major phases, and when did you cut over?
Where did the real-time streaming platform fit in relative to the warehouse migration — before, during, or after?
I want to make sure I've got the timeline straight. Can you lay out, in order, the warehouse cutover, the streaming platform launch, and when governance came in — with rough dates?

candidate_last_answer: Right, so the timeline. We cut the warehouse over in Q3 of 2022 — that was the big bang migration, everything moved at once. The streaming platform actually came first, we'd already had streaming running for about a year before the warehouse, so that would've been... 2023, roughly. And governance, we stood that up early, right at the start, before any of the migration — though honestly a lot of the governance work only happened after the streaming platform was already feeding the warehouse, because that's when the data-quality issues showed up. So governance was first, but also it was really a response to the streaming data, which came before the warehouse, which we cut over in Q3 2022. It all happened pretty fast, within about a year, maybe a year and a half.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: streaming platform actually came first, we'd already had streaming running for about a year before the warehouse

expected-yield: a single self-consistent chronology with concrete dates that resolves the contradiction (streaming dated to 2023 yet 'a year before' a Q3-2022 warehouse cutover, and governance claimed both first and as a response to streaming), pinning what actually happened when
